ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 (hot sauce) is the closest parent category. No specific FoodOn term exists for Japanese spicy sauce as a distinct condiment.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Sauces and similar products) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Japanese spicy ramen sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this condiment category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of spicy sauce as a condiment is approximately 15 g (about one tablespoon), as used alongside ramen or other Japanese dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Hot sauce, ready-to-serve (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 06164)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for hot sauce (ready-to-serve), which is the closest approved-source analogue for a generic Japanese-style spicy sauce condiment. The USDA entry captures the chili-pepper, vinegar, and salt base typical of this condiment class. Sodium and energy values are consistent with this profile. Values are used as a best-available estimate pending a Japan-specific laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: Chili sauce / hot sauce — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate vinegar-chili condiment category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese spicy sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 32 based on the closest analogous food category: vinegar-based chili condiments, which are low-GI due to acetic acid content suppressing postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (32 × 5.0 × 0.15) / 100 = 0.24, rounded to 0.2. This is a low-GI, low-GL condiment.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1250 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit spicy sauce intake, as a single 15 g serving contributes approximately 188 mg sodium, and cumulative condiment use can significantly exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy sauce has a low glycemic index (~32) and provides only approximately 0.75 g of carbohydrate per 15 g serving, making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose elevation when used as a condiment in typical quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the saturated fat content is negligible, the high sodium concentration in spicy sauce poses a cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should use this condiment sparingly and monitor total daily sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy sauce is a pungent, chili-based condiment commonly served alongside Japanese ramen and other noodle dishes. It is typically prepared from a blend of chili peppers, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, vinegar, and salt, resulting in a deeply flavoured, fiery sauce with a sharp, umami-rich taste and a smooth to slightly textured consistency. In the context of Japanese ramen restaurants in Singapore, it is offered as a small side portion to allow diners to adjust the heat level of their broth. Nutritionally, spicy sauce is low in energy (~30 kcal per 100 g) and provides minimal protein and fat, but is notably high in sodium (~1250 mg per 100 g), which is a primary clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its glycemic index is low (~32), and the glycemic load per typical serving is negligible, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used in small quantities. The capsaicin content from chili peppers may offer minor metabolic benefits. Allergens include soy and sesame. This condiment is generally vegan and halal-compatible.
common_user_queries: What is spicy sauce in ramen?
Is spicy sauce high in sodium?
How many calories in spicy sauce?
What allergens are in spicy sauce?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Japanese spicy sauce exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogue (hot sauce, ready-to-serve). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for vinegar-chili condiments. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy hot sauce entry (NDB No. 06164) as the closest approved-source analogue. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for this condiment class (naturally zero by formulation). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based condiment containing no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; plant-based condiment with no vitamin D source. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. GI set to 32 as an analogous estimate for vinegar-based chili condiments per University of Sydney GI Database category; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per 15 g serving calculated as (32 × 5.0 × 0.15) / 100 = 0.24, rounded to 0.2. Source restaurant: Ramen Matsuri, 7 North Canal Road, Singapore (field survey data). Price range: SGD 1.64.
